coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), known for their exceptional optical properties, hold the potential to enhance light absorption, charges separation and transfer in semiconductor materials. However, using cheap medicinal materials as precursors in the field of photocatalysis to obtain CQDs has seldom concerned. In this study, we utilized biomass-derived CQDs from carbon-rich licorice powder to improve photocatalytic CO2 and Cr(VI) reduction in polymer carbon nitride (PCN). The CQDs/g-C3N4 heterojunctions were synthesized via a hydrothermal method, and the successful fabrication and integration of CQDs on g-C3N4 surfaces were confirmed through detailed characterization. The optimal sample with a licorice powder to PCN mass ratio of 0.05:1 ((50CQDs/CN) demonstrates a tripling in CO2 conversion efficiency under simulated sunlight compared to the reference PCN. Under visible light irradiation, the 50CQDs/CN also performs superior photocatalytic Cr (VI) removal efficiency, which is 2.48 times of that of the reference PCN. This significant boost is attributable to the successful construction of the CQDs/CN heterojunction and the abundance of functional groups on the CQDs surface, which provide more active sites, improve light absorption and increase charges migration rates. The stability of these new photocatalysts was confirmed through repeated photocatalytic cycles, indicating their potential for practical applications. Mechanistic studies reveal insights into the enhanced photocatalytic pathways and intermediate conversion processes. Our findings present a novel, efficient and sustainable route to fabricate g-C3N4-based photocatalysts, offering a promising strategy for further mitigate the greenhouse gas crisis and environmental pollution.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), known for their exceptional optical properties, hold the potential to enhance light absorption, charges separation and transfer in semiconductor materials. However, using cheap medicinal materials as precursors in the field of photocatalysis to obtain CQDs has seldom concerned. In this study, we utilized biomass-derived CQDs from carbon-rich licorice powder to improve photocatalytic CO2 and Cr(VI) reduction in polymer carbon nitride (PCN). The CQDs/g-C3N4 heterojunctions were synthesized via a hydrothermal method, and the successful fabrication and integration of CQDs on g-C3N4 surfaces were confirmed through detailed characterization. The optimal sample with a licorice powder to PCN mass ratio of 0.05:1 ((50CQDs/CN) demonstrates a tripling in CO2 conversion efficiency under simulated sunlight compared to the reference PCN. Under visible light irradiation, the 50CQDs/CN also performs superior photocatalytic Cr (VI) removal efficiency, which is 2.48 times of that of the reference PCN. This significant boost is attributable to the successful construction of the CQDs/CN heterojunction and the abundance of functional groups on the CQDs surface, which provide more active sites, improve light absorption and increase charges migration rates. The stability of these new photocatalysts was confirmed through repeated photocatalytic cycles, indicating their potential for practical applications. Mechanistic studies reveal insights into the enhanced photocatalytic pathways and intermediate conversion processes. Our findings present a novel, efficient and sustainable route to fabricate g-C3N4-based photocatalysts, offering a promising strategy for further mitigate the greenhouse gas crisis and environmental pollution.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The increasing demand for green energy sources and the mitigation of CO2-induced global warming have emerged as critical challenges worldwide [1–3]. Thus, prioritizing the development of novel renewable energy methods is of utmost importance. Equally urgent is the need to innovate technologies for high-efficiency CO2 conversion to lower atmospheric CO2 levels and repurpose CO2 into value-added products, thereby achieving a green and efficient closed-loop utilization of CO2 [4]. Meanwhile, the global environmental water pollution control and prevention technology still need to be further strengthened. Especially, lead, mercury, chromium and other heavy metal pollutants control is one of the key issues, in order to pursue green and sustainable development, especially to improve the quality of the ecological environment as the core, pay attention to reduce pollution and carbon synergies [5,6].

Photocatalysis is recognized as one of the most ideal and promising technological solutions [7–9]. The efficacy of this technology hinges on the performance of photocatalysts, which often suffer from low photocatalytic conversion efficiencies, leading to subpar CO2 elimination and heavy-metal degradation hindering commercial viability [10]. The challenge to surmount this bottleneck in CO2 conversion and heavy-metal degradation lies in the design of green photocatalysts that exhibit enhanced activity and durability. Among the semiconductors with high reduction potential, polymer carbon nitride (PCN) has gained prominence due to its affordability, simple production, excellent visible light responsiveness and robust physicochemical stability [11–13]. Despite these advantages, intrinsic PCN is plagued by a limited specific surface area and fast recombination rates of photogenerated charges, resulting in poor adsorption and catalytic reduction efficiency [14,15]. At present, various strategies have been developed to promote the photocatalytic performance of PCN, for instance, morphology regulation [16], noble metal modification [17], heterojunction construction [18], elemental doping [19], and defect engineering [20]. Heterojunction construction stands out as one of the most effective approaches because it can not only expand the spectral absorption range but also modify interfacial charges transfer properties, proving to be practically beneficial in applications, such as heterostructure development [21,22].

Among the vast array of carbonaceous nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are distinguished by their small (usually sub-10 nm) size and their remarkable physical, chemical, and optical attributes. These characteristics render CQDs highly versatile in the fabrication of photocatalysts [23]. Notably, the sources for CQDs are abundantly available, with high-carbon-content compounds being particularly suitable. Biomass materials are frequently employed as precursors for CQDs, leading to the formation of functional photocatalytic heterojunctions when combined with semiconductor materials [24]. For example, Liang et al. synthesized CQDs from ginkgo biloba leaves via hydrothermal treatment and integrated them into PCN nanosheets through thermal condensation of urea. The resultant CQDs/PCN heterojunctions facilitated photogenerated charge transfer and heightened light absorption, which culminated in a photocatalytic H2 evolution rate that was 4.1 times greater than that of the pristine PCN [25]. Similarly, Hu and colleagues prepared chlorine-doped CQDs using mulberry branch powder and sulfoxide chloride, subsequently combining them with g-C3N4. This synthesis yielded a novel metal-free photocatalyst, Cl-CCN, which exhibited enhanced efficiency in both photocatalytic tetracycline degradation and H2 evolution experiments [26]. Interesting, Wang et al. used microwaved milk to synthesize fluorescent CQDs, proved that CQDs with the strong fluorescence can be used in biomedical imaging and many other applications [27]. The common feature is that the performance of the photocatalyst is indelibly related to a variety of functional surface groups on the surface of the introduced CQDs, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino groups, which can optimize the adsorption and resolution mode of the target molecules and accelerate the transfer rate of photogenerated electrons, thus affecting the activity and selectivity of the photocatalyst [28–31]. Nonetheless, posing a pioneering challenge to boost the photocatalytic performance of PCN through a bottom-up synthesis of CQDs and the pursuit of a sustainable and cost-effective method to prepare CQDs/PCN heterojunction.

As a traditional and cheap medicine herb, licorice is derived from the root of the licorice plant (Glycyrrhiza glabra). Chemically, it is a complex mixture of compounds rather than a single chemical entity. The primary active component is glycyrrhizin, also known as glycyrrhizic acid or liquiritin, which is a saponin compound. Glycyrrhizin is responsible for the sweet flavor of licorice, being many times sweeter than sucrose (table sugar). In addition to glycyrrhizin, licorice root contains various other compounds, including flavonoids (like liquiritin, isoliquiritin), isoflavones, chalcones, coumarins, polysaccharides, amino acids. Therefore, it is an interesting approach to synthesize CQDs by hydrothermal method using the rich carbon-based compounds in licorice powder as carbon precursors.

In this context, we synthesized CQDs/PCN heterojunctions utilizing urea and licorice powder derived through a combined thermal condensation and hydrothermal method. This integration not only significantly expanded the specific surface area of PCN but also markedly enhanced its light-responsive properties. Concurrently, construction of heterojunctions optimized the band structure of PCN and enhanced delocalized electron transport. The outstanding performance of CQDs substantially improved the separation and migration rates of photogenerated carriers, effectively diminishing the recombination rate of charge pairs. Optimizing the licorice powder content led to an exemplary CO evolution efficiency of 50CQDs/CN (28.9 μmol·g−1) under simulated sunlight. Meanwhile, 50CQDs/CN performs a higher efficiency in Cr(VI) reduction experiments (0.1529 min−1), in which photogenerated electrons play a significant role. Repeated irradiation tests underscored the photocatalyst’s robustness. Based on our experimental observations, the photocatalytic mechanism of the CQDs/g-C3N4 heterojunctions was analyzed in detail.

The protocol for preparing the samples, outlined in Scheme 1 , begins with the synthesis of polymer carbon nitride (PCN). PCN was synthesized via a conventional thermal condensation of urea, serving as the precursor [32]. To prepare the suspension system A, 1 g of PCN and 0.1 g of sodium dodecyl benzene sulfonate (SDBS) were dispersed in 40 mL of deionized water. The mixture was subjected to intense ultrasonication for 2 h until SDBS dissolved and the PCN was uniformly dispersed. Subsequently, the desired quantity of licorice powder (particle size < 40 mesh) was incorporated into this system and stirred continuously at 25 ℃ for another 2 h. The resulting mixture was then transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. An additional 20 mL of deionized water was used to rinse the beaker, ensuring a total volume of 60 mL in the autoclave. The sealed autoclave was heated to 160 ℃ for 12 h. Upon cooling to ambient temperature, the product was washed and filtered several times with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol, then was dried at 60 ℃ overnight. The dried product was finely ground to yield the final photocatalysts.

The photocatalysts were named according to the mass ratio of licorice powder to g-C3N4, resulting in sample names 10CQDs/CN, 30CQDs/CN, 50CQDs/CN, 70CQDs/CN, and 90CQDs/CN. For instance, the 90CQDs/CN sample represents a mass ratio of 0.09:1 of licorice powder to PCN. The reference PCN sample underwent the same procedure minus the addition of licorice powder. Control experiments were conducted with individual samples of licorice powder, SDBS, and deionized water processed identically as described above.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized separately by adding 5 g of licorice powder to a Teflon-lined autoclave containing 60 mL of deionized water, stirred for 30 min. The sealed system was then maintained at 160 ℃ for 12 h. After the system cooling down to room temperature, the resulting yellow–brown supernatant was heated until a solid formed. This solid was then ground to produce the black CQDs.

The instruments and methods used for the characterization of the photocatalysts were described in our previous work [33,34]. Steady-state photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL, RF-6000) was employed to detect the optical properties of the resulting CQDs and the recombination efficiency of photogenerated carriers in the sample. Ultraviolet photo-electron spectroscopy (UPS) measurement was carried out using a Thermo Fisher ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer.

Photocatalytic CO2 reduction activity of the samples was evaluated using a gas–solid reaction setup. Each sample, 10 mg was uniformly dispersed in a small circular container and placed within a hollow reactor. To act as a reducing agent, 0.5 mL of deionized water was added to the bottom of the reactor. Condensate water, circulated in an intermediary cooling layer, maintained the reaction system at 6 ℃. The temperatures of the vacuum glass tube and the gas inlet were set at 70 ℃ and 100 ℃, respectively, to ensure efficient gas circulation. Before commencing the reaction, the system was purged of air using a vacuum pump, a procedure lasting roughly 20 min. Afterward, 5 mL of high-purity CO2 was introduced. With the activation of a 300 W Xenon lamp, the gases in the system were directed into a gas chromatograph at one-hour intervals, utilizing high-purity N2 as a carrier gas. A flame ionization detector (FID) precisely measured the concentrations of the resulting gases.

Furthermore, the apparent quantum efficiency (AQY) for the photocatalytic reduction of CO2 was determined under irradiation at three distinct monochromatic wavelengths (420 nm, 460 nm, and 520 nm). The AQY was calculated using the following formula:

The photocatalytic performance was further evaluated by Cr (VI) reduction efficiency. In detail, 50 mg photocatalyst was dispersed into 50 mL potassium dichromate solution (K2Cr2O7, 10 mg·L−1). In dark, the suspension was stirred vigorously for 30 min to achieve the equilibrium of absorption–desorption on photocatalyst surface. Under the irradiation of a 300 W Xe lamp (>420 nm), the suspension was sucked out and filtered at an interval of 5 min. The supernatant was added with 100 μL H3PO4 (50 vol%) and H2SO4 (50 vol%) and 400 μL chromogenic agent (2 g/L diphenylcarbazide), respectively. The absorbance of pink solution was measured at 540 nm on a UV–Vis spectrophotometer.

To confirm that licorice powder can yield carbon quantum dots (CQDs) via hydrothermal reaction, the distinctive properties of the resultant CQDs were analyzed. Initially, Fig. 1
A reveals a pronounced absorption peak in the ultraviolet region that extends into the visible spectrum. Additionally, a subtle shoulder is observed between 350–400 nm, likely attributable to the π → π* transition of C=C bonds and the n → π* transition of C=O bonds [35]. It is evident that the water-soluble CQDs exhibit bright fluorescence under illumination (under the excitation of 365 nm UV light lamp), a quintessential photoluminescence feature of CQDs, as depicted in the inset of Fig. 1 A, for the CQDs, the position of emission peaks has no obvious shift when the excitation wavelength changes from 320 to 400 nm, but the emission intensity shows the characteristics of firstly increases and then drops with the increasing of excitation wavelength, which is attributed to the relatively uniform size of CQDs and the same emission sites on its surface, which is affected by the synergistic effect of surface functional groups and carbon cores. Moreover, the CQDs demonstrate up-conversion photoluminescence properties. As illustrated in Fig. 1 B, the CQDs can transform various long-wavelength lights (550 nm-800 nm) into shorter wavelengths (400 nm-480 nm), thereby energizing higher energy states and broadening the light utilization spectrum. However, the shorter excitation wavelength promotes the energy absorbed by the CQDs, resulting in more excited high energy states and lower signal peaks. This interesting phenomenon is attributed to the content of multiple functional groups on the surface of CQDs, nevertheless, at present, there are some controversies about the up-conversion photoluminescence characteristics of CQDs and there is no clear explanation for the up-conversion mechanism [36]. Consequently, the characteristic analyses of CQDs allow us to deduce that licorice powder can indeed produce CQDs through hydrothermal reaction.

The crystal structure of all samples was characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD). Fig. 2
A illustrates that only two characteristic diffraction peaks are evident at 2θ = 12.8° and 27.5°, corresponding to the (100) and (002) crystal plane of PCN, respectively. These can be identified as the planar infinite junctions of the tri-s-triazine ring unit structure and the stacking of nanolayers [37,38]. No diffraction peaks associated with CQDs were discernible in any of the patterns, which is likely a result of either the low content of CQDs or their uniform distribution on the PCN nanolayers. Notably, upon magnifying the diffraction peak of the (002) crystal planes (Fig. 2 B), a subtle shift to higher angles is observed in the samples containing CQDs, increasing with the amount of licorice powder added. A higher diffraction angle, as per the Bragg equation (2dsinθ = nλ), suggests a reduced crystal plane spacing, indicative of a robust interaction between CQDs and PCN due to the decreased stacking distance of the nanolayers [39]. This is advantageous for facilitating the transition of photoinduced e−/h+ pairs between layers, diminishing the migration time from the bulk to the surface, and enhancing photocatalytic activity. Fourier Transform Infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was employed to investigate the functional groups and chemical composition on the surface of the photocatalysts. In Fig. 2 C, the peak at 810 cm−1 is assigned to the out-of-plane bending vibration of the tri-s-triazine units. The region from 1200-1700 cm−1 corresponds to the characteristic vibrations of aromatic C-N, with peaks at 1230 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 in CQDs is attributable to the stretching vibrations of C-O bonds and the bending modes of C=C bonds, respectively [40]. Intriguingly, as the CQD loading increases, the peak intensity at 1230 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 diminishes or vanishes, suggesting a strong interaction between the licorice powder derived CQDs and the aromatic rings on the PCN surface, corroborating the successful loading of formed CQDs on the PCN to form heterojunctions. Moreover, the broad absorption band from 3000-3500 cm−1 relates to the N–H bonds at the edge of the PCN nanolayer and the O–H stretching vibrations of physically adsorbed H2O molecules, indicating that the addition of CQDs introduces additional O–H groups.

The adsorption–desorption characteristics and specific surface areas of all samples were determined via multi-point Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis. The samples exhibit a type IV isotherm with a type H3 hysteresis loop (Fig. 2 D), indicative of mesoporous structures formed by slits or large pores [41]. Such pore architecture is conducive to capturing incident light, enhancing the refractive index and thus improving light utilization. Table 1 lists the specific surface areas of all samples. The reference PCN has a specific surface area of 56.3 m2/g. As the amount of licorice powder increasing, the specific surface area of photocatalysts generally increases, therein 50CQDs/CN having the largest surface area, 20 % greater than that of the reference PCN. This suggests that hydrothermally-generated CQDs may carry in-situ defects, enlarging the exposed space. Typically, a larger specific surface area exposes more active sites and augments target molecule capture capacity, significantly advancing the photocatalytic reduction performance [42].

The micro-morphologies of PCN and 50CQDs/CN were examined using scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Fig. 3
A and 3B show both samples exhibit an irregular layered structure, characterized by the aggregation of nanosheets, with no noticeable difference in appearance before and after CQD modification. Fig. 3 C displays the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of 50CQDs/CN, where numerous small black dots of varying sizes are uniformly distributed across the PCN nanosheets, which is attributable to CQDs, and the average size of particle is about 3.4 nm. Moreover, as illustrated in Fig. 3 D, distinct lattice fringes within the small black dots are visible, with a fringe spacing of 0.21 nm. This measurement matches perfectly with the (100) crystal face of CQDs [43], confirming that CQDs have been synthesized from licorice powder via hydrothermal reaction and successfully loaded onto PCN, forming CQDs/CN heterojunctions.

The surface elemental compositions and chemical valence states of the samples were further probed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. 4
A presents the XPS spectra of the samples, revealing the presence of only C, N, and O elements. Notably, the signal peaks in the 50CQDs/CN are more pronounced than in PCN, suggesting that CQDs with rich surface functional groups were successfully loaded onto PCN. For CQDs, C 1 s can be fitted into three peaks at 288.1 eV, 286.3 eV, and 284.8 eV, corresponding to C=O/C=N, C-OH and C–C/C=C, respectively (Fig. 4 B). The N 1 s spectra of CQDs showed two obvious peaks at 399.8 eV and 401.5 eV, which were associated with N–H and N-C, respectively (Fig. 4 C). As shown in Fig. 4 D, the O 1 s spectra exhibited two strong signal peaks at 533.4 eV and 532.2 eV, corresponding to C-O and C=O, respectively [44–46]. Meanwhile, the high-resolution C 1 s spectrum of PCN can be accurately deconvoluted into two peaks at 284.8 eV and 288.2 eV, corresponding to amorphous C–C and sp2 hybridized C (N-C=N) on the aromatic ring, respectively (Fig. 4 E) [47]. For 50CQDs/CN, the C 1 s peak can be resolved into three peaks, with the one at 286.5 eV can be ascribed to the C-OH bond in the CQDs shift 0.2 eV to the high binding energy after the formation of CQDs/CN heterojunction [48]. Additionally, Fig. 4 F shows the high-resolution XPS N 1 s spectra of both samples can be fitted with three peaks, corresponding to the aromatic ring C-N=C (398.7 eV), the bridge N-C3 bond (400.1 eV), and the amino functional group –NH2 (401.2 eV) [49], respectively. Table 2 illustrates that, compared to PCN, the binding energies of N-C=N peak and C-N=C peak in 50CQDs/CN have shift to low binding energy, which can be attributed to the fact that most CQDs are loaded to the aromatic ring of PCN, and the formation of heterojunction induces a strong electron transfer effect between PCN and CQDs. The relative content of –NH2 in 50CQDs/CN is increased, reflecting the rich amino functional groups in CQDs.

Low-temperature electron paramagnetic resonance spectroscopy (EPR) was utilized to further elucidate the formation of CQDs/CN heterojunction is conducive to the transfer of photogenerated electrons (Fig. 4 G). A distinct Lorentz line at g = 2.003 is observable in all samples, attributable to the paramagnetic signal of unpaired electrons carried by the atoms in the catalyst [50,51]. In the reference PCN, post-urea roasting leads to a minority of atoms possessing unpaired electrons. The heterojunction samples exhibit a more robust paramagnetic signal, indicating that there are abundant defects in the surface functional groups of the loaded CQDs. Uniquely, these surface properties of CQDs can significantly boost the delocalization ability of photocatalysts and promote the migration behavior of photogenerated electrons, thereby enhancing photocatalytic reduction efficiency. As the licorice powder content increasing, the EPR signal intensity correspondingly rises, denoting a higher concentration of CQDs. Nevertheless, an excessive loading of CQDs may lead to overwriting of the original active site or act as recombination centers for carriers, adversely impacting the photocatalytic process and diminishing photocatalytic activity. Besides, using Helium gas (He 1, hv = 21.22 eV) as the photon energy source, the work function of PCN before and after CQDs loading was tested and calculated by UPS. The outcomes were shown in Fig. 4 H- Fig. 4 I. According to the formula Φ = hυ-(Ecutoff-Efermi), the work function of PCN and 50CQDs/CN is 4.92 eV and 4.68 eV. respectively, which strongly proves that construction of CQDs/CN heterojunction reduces the work function of the system, which is conducive to the transfer and migration of photogenerated electrons, promoting the photocatalytic reduction performance.

Optical properties of the synthesized samples were characterized by UV–Vis diffuse reflection spectroscopy (UV–Vis DRS). Fig. 5
A demonstrates that the maximum absorption edge for all samples is positioned at approximately 430 nm, and the pristine CQDs has strong light absorption over the full wavelength range. Relative to PCN, the light absorption intensity of all CQDs/CN samples is enhanced in the 430–800 nm range, with the absorption strength incrementally intensifying as the amount of licorice powder increasing. This enhancement is attributable to the rising concentration of CQDs with strong light absorption properties, which is consistent with the observed color change in the prepared photocatalysts (light yellow → dark yellow). Furthermore, Fig. 5 B and Fig. 5 C shows the band gap of PCN and CQDs was calculated to be 2.7 eV and 1.64 eV using the equation (αhv)1/n = A(hv-Eg), n = 2 or 1/2, respectively, the band gap obtained is close to the previously results obtained [52–54].

To deepen the understanding of the photocatalytic reduction mechanism, both the XPS valence band (VB) spectrum and the Mott-Schottky plot were acquired. As illustrated in Fig. 5 D, the XPS-VB spectrum precisely determines the VB positions of the samples, with the reference PCN and CQDs at 1.58 V and 0.76 V, respectively. Employing the equation ECB=EVB − Eg, the conduction band (CB) edge positions for PCN and CQDs is identified as −1.12 V and −0.88 V, respectively. Additionally, the Mott-Schottky results, indicated by positive slope curves in Fig. 5 E, confirming the n-type semiconductor nature of the samples [21,55]. The flat-band potential (Efb) of the samples is the intersection of the curve and the X-axis, which is −1.0 V and −1.09 V, respectively. Typically, the Efb of n-type semiconductors is 0.1–0.3 V more positive than the CB potential [56,57], corroborating the CB potential positions determined by the XPS VB spectrum. Besides, the lower the slope of the M−S curve, the higher the calculated carriers density [58,59]. Therefore, the slope of the M−S curve of 50CQDs/CN is lower than that of PCN, confirming that the formation of CQDs/CN heterojunction can significantly increase the photogenerated carriers density. A schematic of the band structures was depicted in Fig. 5 F. The CB potentials of all samples are sufficiently negative to exceed the conversion potentials required for the reduction of CO2 to CO and CH4, which substantiates that the developed photocatalyst satisfies the thermodynamic prerequisites for photocatalytic CO2 reduction.

The influence of the CQDs/CN heterojunction construction on the separation and migration of photogenerated carriers was further explored through photoelectric measurements. Fig. 6
A displays the surface photovoltage (SPV) signals of all samples. PCN exhibits a robust response signal in the UV light region (300–400 nm) and a weaker visible light response from 400 to 550 nm. Compared to PCN, all CQD/CN samples show enhanced optical response signals, with 50CQDs/CN presenting the highest and broadest optical response. Generally, a stronger SPV signal indicates more rapid separation and migration of photoexcited carriers, which significantly improves the photocatalytic reduction rate [60]. Concurrently, the transient photocurrent densities were recorded through five cycles of light exposure (Fig. 6 B), revealing stable photocurrent signals after 200 s, with those of CQDs/CN samples notably surpassing PCN. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) shows that CQDs/CN samples possess a smaller arc radius than PCN, suggesting lower charges transfer resistance (Fig. 6 C). Consequently, CQDs/CN heterojunctions facilitate more efficient photoexcited carriers separation and migration, which grants the heterojunction enhanced photocatalytic reduction activity [61].

Cyclic voltammetry was performed to evaluate the double-layer capacitance and, thus, the electrochemical active surface area (EASA) of the photocatalysts (Fig. 6 D). The slope of the double-layer capacitance fitting curve for 50CQDs/CN is 83.3 μF·cm−2, which is 4.58 times greater than that of PCN (18.2 μF·cm−2). This demonstrates that the incorporation of CQDs notably increases the EASA of PCN, thereby exposing more active sites [62]. Additionally, the steady-state photoluminescence (PL) spectra of all samples, excited at 345 nm, were depicted in Fig. 6 E. The emission peaks are observed at 460 nm for all samples, with a lower PL signal correlating with reduced recombination efficiency of photogenerated carriers, aligning with the SPV results. Finally, time-resolved photoluminescence (TR-PL) spectra were analyzed to assess the dynamics of photogenerated carriers (Fig. 6 F). The average lifetime of photogenerated carriers in 50CQDs/CN is shorter than in PCN, implying that the CQDs/CN heterojunction markedly expedite carriers migration, thereby benefiting photocatalytic reduction efficiency.

In the photocatalytic gas–solid CO2 reduction process, the adsorption of CO2 and the desorption of products over the catalyst are critical factors. Therefore, CO2 physical adsorption was measured. As depicted in Fig. 7
A, 50CQDs/CN demonstrates a superior CO2 adsorption capacity compared to PCN across various pressures. The desorption characteristics of the photocatalyst were also examined using CO2 temperature-programmed desorption (TPD). Fig. 7 B reveals that both samples exhibit prominent desorption peaks at approximately 140 ℃ and 405 ℃. Notably, the desorption capacity of 50CQDs/CN surpasses that of PCN, suggesting that 50CQDs/CN can more rapidly release the reacted products from its surface, thereby enabling efficient CO2 conversion [63]. Both experimental outcomes strongly substantiate that the formation of CQDs/CN heterojunctions significantly enhance the CO2 adsorption and desorption performance of the photocatalyst, providing a robust basis for its exceptional photocatalytic reduction activity.

The photocatalytic CO2 reduction activity of all samples was assessed through a gas–solid reaction under a 300 W Xe lamp for 4 h. Fig. 8
A illustrates the CO evolution efficiency of all samples post-photocatalytic reaction for 4 h. PCN converts CO2 to CO at a relatively stable rate among the samples. During the initial hour, the CO2 conversion efficiency over the CQDs/CN samples is low, potentially due to the activation period required for the photocatalysts. In the subsequent three hours, the conversion efficiency markedly improves, with the yield continuing to rise at a steady rate. It is computed that the CO2 evolution rate over PCN is 2.4 μmol·g−1·h−1 (Fig. 8 B). As the CQDs content incrementally increases, so does the catalytic activity. The peak CO2 conversion efficiency is observed with 50CQDs/CN, reaching 7.2 μmol·g−1·h−1, which is triple that of PCN. Nonetheless, an overabundance of CQD decoration leads to a decline in catalytic activity, aligning with the photoelectric property findings. The modification of g-C3N4 with CQDs, bestows upon PCN a larger specific surface area and enhanced CO2 adsorption capacity. This, in turn, elevates light absorption and electrochemical reduction efficiency by significantly fostering the separation and migration of photogenerated carriers, culminating in optimized photocatalytic CO2 reduction efficiency. Meanwhile, blank experiments were performed to detect the true source of the CO produced (Fig. 8 C). The results showed that no CO could be produced in the absence of catalyst, light, water or CO2, indicating that the carbon of CO only comes from the injected CO2 and not from the self-decomposition of PCN. Additionally, SPV test results indicate that the samples also respond to visible light; hence, the apparent quantum yield (AQY) of CO2 reduction was examined and computed under three distinct wavelengths of monochromatic light (Fig. 8 D). The AQY of CO evolution over 50CQDs/CN under 420 nm illumination is 1.36 × 10−3, approximately fivefold that over PCN (0.27 × 10−3). Compared to PCN, the AQY of CO evolution on 50CQDs/CN under 460 nm and 520 nm light is roughly double that of PCN. Furthermore, separate experiments were conducted to discern the impact of additives. As shown in Fig. 8 E, the photocatalytic CO2 reduction activity of PCN is modest following a hydrothermal reaction with only deionized water, yet significantly enhanced with SDBS or licorice powder. The photocatalytic CO2 reduction activity of PCN is notably augmented following hydrothermal treatment with both SDBS and licorice powder.

Photocatalytic activity of the prepared samples was further investigated by photocatalytic reduction of Cr(VI) under visible light irradiation. As exhibited in Fig. 9
A, the removal rate of Cr(VI) on PCN and 50CQDs/CN is 71.3 % and 99.5 % after 20 min, respectively. The color evolution results of the residual solution after different illumination time were presented in Fig. 9 A. The removal of Cr(VI) on photocatalysts fits well with a pseudo-first-order kinetic (Fig. 9 B). Apparently, 50CQDs/CN displays higher photocatalytic reduction efficiency compared with PCN, the removal rate constant of Cr(VI) over 50CQDs/CN was calculated to be 0.1529 min−1, which is 2.48-fold of that of over the PCN (k = 0.0617 min−1). Fig. 9 C shows the evolution process of rapid removal of Cr(VI) by continuous irradiation over two photocatalysts. The main active species during Cr(VI) removal were further investigated through capture experiments. Commonly, benzoquinone (BQ), ammonium oxalate (AO) and potassium bromate (KBrO3) are used as trapping agents toward superoxide radicals (·O2 −), photogenerated holes (h+) and photogenerated electrons (e−), separately. As exhibited in Fig. 9 D, addition of BQ has no significant effect on the removal of Cr (VI) after illumination for 20 min. Interestingly, addition of AO can promote the reduction of Cr(VI), which can be attributed to the fact that AO can trap holes in the system and promote the separation of photogenerated carriers, thus producing more photogenerated electrons. However, addition of KBrO3 significantly inhibits the removal of Cr(VI), and the removal rate of Cr(VI) is only 15.9 %, which strongly indicates that the photogenerated electrons are the most critical active species in the removal process of Cr(VI) [64].

Optimizing the catalytic activity of photocatalysts is a primary objective of their preparation, and ensuring their stability is equally crucial. For this reason, 50CQDs/CN was chosen as a representative to undergo four consecutive cycles of photocatalytic CO2 reduction experiments. The results, as depicted in Fig. 10
A, show that the photocatalytic CO2 reduction activity of 50CQDs/CN remains high even after 16 h of continuous light exposure. Additionally, X-ray diffraction analysis indicates that the crystal structure of the catalyst remains unchanged post-reaction compared to the fresh sample (Fig. 10 B). Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra reveal that the peaks at 1230 and 1400 cm−1, which had vanished, re-emerge after cycling (Fig. 10 C). This could be due to the diminished interaction between CQDs and PCN during the CO2 reduction process, implying that the interaction between CQDs and PCN is stable. The outcomes of the cyclic experiments robustly affirm that the synthesized samples not only possess outstanding photocatalytic reduction activity but also exhibit excellent durability. Comparative analysis of the photocatalytic reduction activity of the synthesized photocatalyst with other similar materials was presented in Table 3 . The catalytic performance of 50CQDs/CN surpasses that of other photocatalysts, suggesting that this material holds promising potential for practical applications.

In-situ diffuse reflectance infrared Fourier transform spectroscopy (DRIFTS) was employed to examine the chemical groups on the catalyst surface during the reaction process (Fig. 10 D). No species were detected after 30 min in dark, but upon continuous light exposure, various peaks appeared successively, indicating that CO2 molecules experience multiple transformations under light before being converted into CO. The absorption peak at 1717 cm−1 is attributable to adsorbed CO2, and peaks around 1424 cm−1 and 1313 cm−1 correspond to monodentate and bidentate carbonates, respectively [65]. A broad absorption range from 1485-1574 cm−1 is ascribed to the characteristic peaks of COOH*. Consequently, the intensity of these intermediate characteristic peak’s changes with the duration of irradiation time, allowing the pathway of photocatalytic CO2 reduction to be inferred as depicted in Scheme 2 .

Integrating all the presented analyses, a convincing mechanism for photocatalytic reduction of CO2 and removal of Cr(VI) was devised to provide a clearer understanding of the photoinduced charges transfer pathways. As depicted in Fig. 11 , upon exposure to light, electrons in the valence band of CQDs/CN photocatalysts are excited and jump to the conduction band, absorbing sufficient energy and leaving behind holes in the valence band. The conduction band edge potential of PCN is more negative than the CO reduction potential, so numerous photoexcited electrons can directly reduce CO2 adsorbed on the surface to CO and reduce Cr(VI) to Cr(III) [66]. Notably, formation of CQDs/CN heterojunctions leverages the superior optical properties and surface functional groups of CQDs, which markedly accelerates the transfer of excited electrons. This, in turn, improves the utilization of photogenerated electrons, boosts the CO production rate and enhances removal efficiency of Cr(VI) [67–69].

In conclusion, the fabrication of CQDs/g-C3N4 heterojunctions using a precise ratio of biomass-derived licorice powder has been effective. Systematic materials characterization affirms the successful integration and loaded of CQDs, enhancing the specific surface area and photoelectric properties of g-C3N4. This advancement has significantly boosted its photocatalytic reduction capabilities. After four hours of simulated sunlight irradiation, the CO evolution efficiency on CQDs/g-C3N4 surpassed that of the reference polymer carbon nitride (PCN). Specifically, the 50CQDs/g-C3N4 variant achieved the highest activity (28.9 μ mol·g−1), tripling the efficiency of the reference PCN. The 50CQDs/CN also exhibits ascendant photocatalytic activity than the reference PCN in photocatalytic Cr (VI) removal under visible light irradiation. Further reinforcing its potential, cyclic tests confirmed the heterojunctions photocatalysts' remarkable activity and stability. This study presents an effective strategy to amplify the photocatalytic CO2 reduction and Cr (VI) removal of g-C3N4, and demonstrates a promising step forward in the quest for green and affordable energy solutions, underscoring the synergy of environmental stewardship and innovative material science.

Zhengdong Xu: Writing – original draft. Xun Su: Investigation. Penghui Yang: Investigation. Junbo Zhong: Funding acquisition. Minjiao Li: Supervision. Clemens Burda: Writing – review & editing. Lin Dou: Investigation.
